1: davar YHWH asher haya el Tzefanyahu: 'The word of the LORD that came to Zephaniah.' The prophet's name Tzefanyahu (H6846) combines tzafan (to hide, treasure, protect) with YHWH — meaning 'YHWH has hidden/protected' or 'YHWH's hidden one.' His genealogy (ben Kushi ben Gedalyahu ben Amarya ben Hizqiyahu) goes back four generations — unusual depth suggesting the final Hizqiyahu is the king Hezekiah, making Zephaniah a member of the royal family. The date formula places him in the reign of Josiah (640-609 BCE), before Josiah's reform (621 BCE) based on the still-existing Baal cult described in v4. No other classical prophet claims royal ancestry so clearly.
2: asof asif kol me'al penei ha'adamah: 'I will utterly sweep away everything from the face of the ground.' The doubled root asaf-asof (infinitive absolute + verb, H622) is the Hebrew intensification device: to gather/sweep away — utterly, completely, without remainder. This is the grammar of comprehensive judgment: the double infinitive absolute leaves no room for partial judgment. The scope — everything from the face of the ground (kol me'al penei ha'adamah) — echoes the Flood language (Gen 7:4, 23: every living thing from the face of the ground). YHWH's cosmic judgment is framed in Flood imagery: the creation that was swept away in water (Gen 6-9) is now to be swept away by the Day of the LORD.
3: asif adam uvehemah asif of hashamayim vedegei hayam vehamachshelot et harsha'im: 'I will sweep away man and beast; I will sweep away the birds of the heavens and the fish of the sea, and the rubble with the wicked.' The inverse creation order in vv2-3 (fish, birds, beasts, man — the reverse of Gen 1:20-27) deliberately recalls the un-creation of the Flood: what YHWH created in reverse sequence will be un-created in reverse sequence. The word hamachshelot (the stumbling blocks) is debated — either 'rubble' (ruins of destruction) or 'stumbling blocks' (the idols and causes of sin). The comprehensive judgment reaches to the animal kingdom: not because animals sin but because de-creation of human civilization reverberates through the entire created order (cf. Rom 8:20-22).
4: venatatiy yadiy al Yehudah veal kol yoshvei Yerushalayim... shear Baal vehem hakomerim: 'I will stretch out my hand against Judah and against all the inhabitants of Jerusalem ... the remnant of Baal and the name of the idolatrous priests.' Komer (H3649, pl. komerim) is used specifically for non-Levitical, idolatrous priests — black-robed ministers of Baal and foreign cults (also 2 Kgs 23:5; Hos 10:5). YHWH will cut off 'the remnant of Baal' — Baal worship that persisted in Jerusalem despite earlier reform attempts. The phrase 'the name of the komerim' means: their very memory will be erased (shem = name, memory, reputation). The reform vocabulary (cutting off, remnant) is covenant-judgment language.
5: vehamishttachavim al gaggot litzva hashamayim vehamishtachavim hanishshavim lADONAI umeishavim beMalcham: 'Those who bow down on the rooftops to the host of heaven, those who bow down and swear to the LORD and also swear by Milcom.' The syncretism described — worshiping YHWH while simultaneously swearing by Milcom (the Ammonite deity, also spelled Moloch) — is the prophet's target: not pure paganism but religious double allegiance. Rooftop astral worship was common in the ancient Near East (cf. Jer 19:13; 32:29); the 'host of heaven' (tzva hashamayim) = the stars and planets deified in Babylonian and Canaanite religion. The syncretism violates the first commandment: YHWH will not share allegiance (Deut 6:4-5).
6: vehansogeym me'achar YHWH va'asher lo biqqeshu et YHWH velo darshuhu: 'Those who have turned back from following the LORD, who do not seek the LORD or inquire of him.' The three participles characterize the spiritually indifferent: those who turned away (nasog, H5472 — to draw back, retreat), those who do not seek (biqqesh, H1245 — to search), those who do not inquire of (darash, H1875 — to consult). The vocabulary is Deuteronomic — the Shema commands total seeking of YHWH (Deut 4:29; 6:4-5); Zephaniah condemns those who have abandoned this seeking. Not seeking is as culpable as active apostasy.
7: has millifnei Adonai YHWH ki qarov yom YHWH: 'Hush/Be silent before the Lord GOD! For the day of the LORD is near.' The interjection has (H2013, 'silence!' or 'hush!') appears as a call to reverent, terrified silence before imminent divine action (cf. Hab 2:20; Zech 2:13; Amos 6:10). It is the opposite of festive noise — YHWH has prepared his sacrifice (zevach) and consecrated his guests: the Babylonians or Assyrians as instruments of his judgment. The Day of the LORD language connects to the dominant theme of the entire chapter.
8: vehaya beyom zevach YHWH ufaqadti al hassarim veal benei hamelech veal kol halvoshim malbush nokhri: 'And on the day of the LORD's sacrifice, I will punish the officials and the king's sons and all who array themselves in foreign attire.' The phrase malbush nokhri (foreign garment) likely refers to the adoption of foreign cultural dress as a sign of cultural assimilation — Assyrian or Egyptian court fashion. Clothing as cultural identity marker: to wear foreign clothes is to align oneself with foreign values (cf. 2 Kgs 10:22). The officials and royal family are targeted first — judgment begins at the top of the social hierarchy.
9: ufaqadti al kol hadoleg al hamiftan bayom hahu: 'I will punish all who leap over the threshold on that day, who fill their master's house with violence and fraud.' The strange practice of 'leaping over the threshold' may refer to the Philistine custom of not treading on the threshold of Dagon's temple (1 Sam 5:5), adopted by those in high places as a superstitious ritual — or it may refer to those who burst violently into others' homes to loot them. Either way, the combination of superstitious religious practice and violent economic exploitation characterizes the ruling class YHWH will judge.
10: vehaya bayom hahu neum YHWH qol tzeaqah mishaar hadagim veyelalah min hamishne: 'On that day, declares the LORD, a cry will be heard from the Fish Gate, a wail from the Second Quarter.' The Fish Gate (shaar hadagim) was in the northern wall of Jerusalem — the direction from which Babylonian invasion would come. The Second Quarter (hamishne, the new town, where Huldah the prophetess lived, 2 Kgs 22:14) is also in the northern sector. The geography of the judgment cry traces the invasion route: north to south, quarter by quarter, into the city's heart.
11: heilu yoshvei hamakhtesh ki nidmah kol am Kenaan nikhresu kol nitelay kesef: 'Wail, O inhabitants of the Mortar! For all the traders are no more; all who weigh out silver are destroyed.' Hamakhtesh (H4389 — the Mortar, a hollowed-out place) is the commercial quarter of Jerusalem, likely a valley used as a market. The am Kenaan (people of Canaan) here means 'merchants' — because Canaan (especially Phoenicia) was synonymous with trade in the ancient world; the term becomes a metonym for the commercial class. The merchants are destroyed: the economic engine of Jerusalem's prosperity will be silenced.
12: vehaya baaet hahi achappes et Yerushalayim banerot ufaqadti al ha'anashim haqefuim al shemarihem: 'At that time I will search Jerusalem with lamps, and I will punish the men who are complacent, who say in their hearts, the LORD will not do good, nor will he do ill.' YHWH will search the city with lamps (nerot) — thorough investigation that leaves no hiding place. Those settled on their dregs (qefuim al shemarihem, literally 'thickening on their lees') is a wine metaphor: wine left undisturbed on its sediment becomes thick and undrinkable; the complacent people of Jerusalem have become spiritually stagnant. Their theology is practical deism: YHWH will not intervene, neither for good nor ill — the opposite of prophetic confidence.
13: vehaya cheilam limeshissa uvateihem lishemamah: 'Their goods shall be plundered, and their houses laid waste.' The ironic reversal echoes Deut 28:30, 39 (covenant curses): you will build a house but not live in it; you will plant a vineyard but not drink its wine. The possession-without-enjoyment pattern is the covenant-curse structure: human effort and acquisition, under divine judgment, yields nothing but loss. The prosperous who said YHWH neither punishes nor rewards will discover that their prosperity was only borrowed time.
14: qarov yom YHWH hagadol qarov umahher meod qol yom YHWH mar tzeracha sham gibbor: 'The great day of the LORD is near, near and hastening fast; the sound of the day of the LORD is bitter; the mighty man cries aloud there.' The triple occurrence of 'near' (qarov ... qarov umahher meod) intensifies the urgency. Yom YHWH hagadol (the great Day of the LORD) — the emphatic form distinguishing the eschatological culmination from immediate historical judgments. Even the mighty man (gibbor) cries in bitterness: military strength offers no protection on the Day of YHWH's wrath. Verses 14-16 are the OT's most concentrated Dies Irae passage, providing vocabulary for the medieval Latin hymn Dies Irae, Dies Illa.
15: yom evra hayom hahu yom tzara umetzuka yom shoa umeshuah yom choshekh va'afelah yom anan va'arafel: 'A day of wrath is that day, a day of distress and anguish, a day of ruin and devastation, a day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness.' The six paired qualities of the Day create the OT's most detailed catalog of divine judgment characteristics. Evrah (H5678, wrath/overflow of anger) is the boiling-over of righteous indignation. The darkness imagery (choshekh/afelah, anan/arafel) echoes the Sinai theophany (Exod 19:16; 20:21: the thick darkness where God was) and Amos 5:18-20 (the Day of the LORD is darkness, not light).
16: yom shofar uteruah al he'arim habetzurot veal hapinnot hagvohot: 'A day of trumpet blast and battle cry against the fortified cities and against the lofty battlements.' The shofar (ram's horn trumpet) in military context signals the advance of the divine army; teruah (battle shout/alarm blast) is the covenant battle cry of YHWH's war (cf. Num 10:9; Josh 6:5). The strongest human defenses — fortified cities (he'arim habetzurot) and lofty battlements (ha-pinnot hagvohot) — are explicitly targeted: no human construction withstands the Day of YHWH (cf. Isa 2:15, the Day of the LORD is against every high tower and every fortified wall).
17: vehatzerati la'adam vehalekhu kaivrim ki lADONAI chata'u veshuphach damam ke'afar ulevesaram kegelalim: 'I will bring distress on mankind, so that they shall walk like the blind, because they have sinned against the LORD; their blood shall be poured out like dust, and their flesh like dung.' Walking like the blind (kaivrim) is the covenant curse of Deut 28:29 (you will grope at midday as the blind grope in darkness). The comparison of spilled blood to dust and corpses to dung is the ultimate language of human degradation: the proud and the mighty reduced to filth. The cause: ki lADONAI chata'u (because they sinned against the LORD) — judgment precisely calibrated to covenant violation.
18: gam kaspam gam zahabam lo yuchal lehatzil otam beyom evrat YHWH: 'Neither their silver nor their gold shall be able to deliver them on the day of the LORD's wrath.' The futility of material wealth on the Day of Wrath is the prophetic critique of economic trust: silver and gold — the ultimate measures of human security in the ancient world — are specifically useless in the face of divine judgment. The phrase echoes Ezek 7:19 (their silver and gold cannot deliver them in the day of the wrath of the LORD). The contrast Peter draws: ransomed not with silver or gold but with the precious blood of Christ (1 Pet 1:18-19) — what the Day of Wrath requires cannot be purchased; it must be provided by divine self-sacrifice.
